channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: A sugar key to the origins of life has been discovered in interstellar space - CNN
item.description:
1. A sugar key to the origins of life has been discovered in interstellar space  CNN
  2. A Sweet Surprise: Scientists Find Sugar Deep in Our Galaxy  The New York Times
  3. Space jam: astronomers detect ‘raspberry sugar’ on dust cloud in Milky Way  The Guardian
  4. Detection of a four-carbon sugar in interstellar space  Nature
  5. In a sweet discovery, astronomers find sugar lurking in the space between stars  NBC News

item.description:

  4. We finally know the name of a Maya mathematician  Scientific American

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces  Space Daily
  2. A mysterious “red dot” spotted in space: are we witnessing a cosmic birth?  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  3. How the Webb Telescope is Influencing Astrophysicists’ Theories  Adafruit
  4. Backyard Black Holes  Astrobites
  5. Black holes buried in mysterious 'little red dot' galaxies could blast cosmic ghosts at Earth  Space

item.title: Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman - The New York Times
item.description:
1. Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman  The New York Times

  3. 'Silly sprinklers' put in reverse to further unravel decades-old physics puzzle  Phys.org
  4. NYU Researchers Identify Physical Mechanisms Behind Reverse Sprinkler Motion  geneonline.com

  4. UIUC And ISTA Demonstrate Steady Entanglement Of Qubits  Quantum Zeitgeist

item.title: Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future  Gizmodo
  2. Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas  Space Daily
  3. Researchers confirm cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Stanford Doerr School of Sustainability
  4. Scientists finally solved the mystery of Earth's greatest mass extinction  ScienceDaily
  5. Metabolic vulnerability confirmed as cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Open Access Government

item.title: After Nearly 50 Years In The Depths Of Space, Voyager 1 Still Sends Messages That Take 23 Hours To Reach Earth - Jalopnik
item.description:
1. After Nearly 50 Years In The Depths Of Space, Voyager 1 Still Sends Messages That Take 23 Hours To Reach Earth  Jalopnik
  2. Voyager's signal now reaches Earth as a whisper measured in fractions of a billionth of a watt, fainter than almost anything our antennas were built to hear  Space Daily
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  5. One Light-Day Away: Voyager's Epic Voyage  Rediff

item.title: Scientists examining samples returned from asteroid Bennu found ribose and glucose inside — and with phosphate and every RNA nucleobase already detected in the same asteroid material, every chemical component needed to build RNA was present in th - Space Daily
item.description:

1. Frame-dragging observations validate Einstein yet again  Phys.org

  4. Armenian scientist co-authors Nature study confirming Einstein’s theory with record precision  Public Radio of Armenia

item.title: Six massive landslides discovered on icy Pluto - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Six massive landslides discovered on icy Pluto  Phys.org
  2. Pluto has landslides  Science News
  3. 6 massive landslides discovered on Pluto, one of them twice the size of Manhattan | Earth's landslides are driven heavily by gravity | Inshorts  Inshorts
  4. Traces of massive landslides detected on Pluto  Universe Space Tech
  5. Pluto has six confirmed landslides now, and their unusually long runouts are giving scientists a clue about how icy worlds move  The Times of India

item.title: In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w  Space Daily
  2. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  3. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily
  4. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily

item.title: Weird Mountains on Pluto’s Largest Moon Charon Reveals a Surprising Past - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Weird Mountains on Pluto’s Largest Moon Charon Reveals a Surprising Past  Gizmodo

  4. Pluto's Moon Charon Shows Evidence Of "Despinning" – What Does This Mean?  IFLScience

item.title: Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update - autoevolution
item.description:
1. Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update  autoevolution
  2. A spacecraft called DART was deliberately crashed into an asteroid in 2022 to test whether humanity could deflect one, and it worked — the asteroid's orbit changed measurably, the first time humans have intentionally altered the movement of a natural body in  ScienceBlog.com
  3. NASA mission changed orbit of asteroid around Sun  The Grainger College of Engineering
  4. What Hera Will Find At The Asteroid DART Crashed Into  WorldAtlas

item.title: New Horizons flew past Pluto so quickly that an encounter nine years in the making was effectively over within hours — but the data gathered during that brief passage took another 15 months to trickle home - Space Daily
item.description:
1. New Horizons flew past Pluto so quickly that an encounter nine years in the making was effectively over within hours — but the data gathered during that brief passage took another 15 months to trickle home  Space Daily
  2. On This Day: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  upi.com
  3. NASA’s New Horizons travelled 3 billion miles over 9 years to reach Pluto – but it was moving too fast to stop when it arrived  The Times of India
  4. On This Day, July 14: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  Yahoo
  5. When the New Horizons spacecraft flew past Pluto in July of 2015 after a nine-and-a-half-year journey from Earth, it was traveling so fast — roughly 32,000 miles per hour — that it had less than 30 minutes to photograph the entire dwarf planet, and the images  Space Daily

item.description:

  4. What would happen to a human being on the surface of Venus?  Universe Space Tech

item.title: Einstein’s relativity allows time travel into the future, and we have already measured it: atomic clocks flown around Earth on aeroplanes came home showing a different time from clocks left on the ground - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Einstein’s relativity allows time travel into the future, and we have already measured it: atomic clocks flown around Earth on aeroplanes came home showing a different time from clocks left on the ground  Space Daily
  2. Physics says time travel into the future is not a thought experiment — every astronaut who has orbited Earth has returned a fraction of a second younger than they would have been if they had stayed on the ground  Space Daily
  3. The wild physics that could actually be used to build a time machine  New Scientist
  4. Scientists discovered time travel may already be happening  MSN

item.title: Researchers froze the simplest amino acid to nearly minus 260°C and bombarded it with radiation that mimics cosmic rays — and it formed peptides, the building blocks of proteins, under conditions that exist before stars and planets are born - Space Daily
item.description: Researchers froze the simplest amino acid to nearly minus 260°C and bombarded it with radiation that mimics cosmic rays — and it formed peptides, the building blocks of proteins, under conditions that exist before stars and planets are born  Space Daily
